theme.title: Direct and Indirect Objects
theme.description: Practice direct object pronouns, indirect object pronouns, and combinations of both in practical Spanish.

lesson.title: Lesson 5: Review and Contrast
lesson.instructions: Review direct objects, indirect objects, and double-object combinations in both directions.

exercises.prompt: I know Pedro, but I do not know his sisters.
exercises.answer: Conozco a Pedro, pero no conozco a sus hermanas.
exercises.explanation: This contrast keeps the direct objects as full nouns.

exercises.prompt: Lo conozco, pero no le hablo mucho.
exercises.answer: I know him, but I do not talk to him much.
exercises.explanation: Compare direct object 'lo' with indirect object 'le'.

exercises.prompt: Did you send Sofia the documents? Yes, I sent them to her this morning.
exercises.answer: Le enviaste los documentos a Sofia? Si, se los envie esta manana.
exercises.explanation: The answer uses both indirect and direct object pronouns.

exercises.prompt: No quiero comprarlas porque ya te las prestaron.
exercises.answer: I do not want to buy them because they already lent them to you.
exercises.answer_alt: I do not want to buy them because someone already lent them to you.
exercises.explanation: This sentence mixes a direct object with a double-object structure.

exercises.prompt: My parents are going to give me the car for the weekend, but first they have to clean it.
exercises.answer: Mis padres me van a prestar el carro para el fin de semana, pero primero tienen que limpiarlo.
exercises.answer_alt: Mis padres me van a dar el coche para el fin de semana, pero primero tienen que limpiarlo.
exercises.explanation: The first clause uses an indirect object; the second replaces the car with a direct object pronoun.

exercises.prompt: Si ves a Laura, puedes darle esto? Si la veo, se lo doy.
exercises.answer: If you see Laura, can you give this to her? If I see her, I will give it to her.
exercises.explanation: The first sentence uses an attached indirect object pronoun and the answer uses both object pronouns.

exercises.prompt: The doctor explained the results to us, but he did not explain them to our family.
exercises.answer: El doctor nos explico los resultados, pero no se los explico a nuestra familia.
exercises.explanation: The second clause requires 'se los' for 'to them' plus 'the results'.

exercises.prompt: Te las puedo devolver manana si quieres.
exercises.answer: I can give them back to you tomorrow if you want.
exercises.explanation: 'Te las' combines indirect and direct objects in a practical sentence.
